title: Caregiver-infant interactions selectively shape emerging functional connectivity in the neonatal brain
authors: L. Carnevali, B. Blanco, M. Rozhko, M. Weatherhead, M. H. Johnson, S. Lloyd-Fox
statistical_approach: Functional connectivity analysis, linear mixed models, dyadic interaction coding
abstract: This study combined observations of caregiver-infant interactions at one month with repeated home-based brain imaging using fNIRS. Variations in dyadic engagement and affectionate touch influenced the organization and development of infant brain functional connectivity. Affectionate caregiver-infant interactions were associated with stronger functional connectivity, particularly within the social brain networks.

title: Cortical reconstruction and anatomical parcellation of high-resolution multi-modal postmortem ex vivo MRI of the human infant brain
authors: Pulkit Khandelwal, Sala Young, Nathan Xi Ngo, Paul A. Yushkevich, Andre van der Kouwe, Robin L. Haynes, Hannah C. Kinney, Lilla Zöllei
institution: Massachusetts General Hospital / Harvard Medical School
statistical_approach: Deep learning volumetric segmentation, PostSynth synthetic data generation, cortical surface reconstruction
abstract: This study introduces a multi-modal high-resolution postmortem infant MRI dataset and unified computational framework combining deep learning volumetric segmentation with surface-based cortical reconstruction. PostSynth, a postmortem-specific synthetic data generation engine, explicitly models fixation-driven imaging characteristics including altered gray-white matter contrast, laminar cortical intensity heterogeneity, and specimen-specific bias fields. The framework enables automated anatomical segmentation and cortical surface reconstruction in postmortem infant brains.

title: Neonatal Resting-State Functional Connectivity Predicts Socioemotional and Behavioral Outcomes at 18 Months
authors: Mi Zou, Arun L. W. Bokde
statistical_approach: Stability-driven ROI-constrained connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM), Developing Human Connectome Project
abstract: This study examined whether neonatal functional connectivity predicts 18-month behavioral and temperament outcomes in 397 infants (277 term-born, 120 preterm-born) using resting-state fMRI data from the Developing Human Connectome Project. Outcomes were assessed using the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) and Early Childhood Behavior Questionnaire (ECBQ). Significant predictive models were found for externalizing, internalizing, surgency, negative affect, and effortful control, with clear differences in predictive architecture between term-born and preterm-born groups. Prefrontal and temporoparietal systems were repeatedly implicated.

title: Explicitly nonlinear fMRI networks reveal hidden trajectories of infant brain development
authors: Spencer Kinsey, Geethanjali Nagaboina, Prerana Bajracharya, Masoud Seraji, Zening Fu, Vince D. Calhoun, Sarah Shultz, Armin Iraji
institution: Georgia State University / Emory University / TReNDS Center
statistical_approach: Data-driven nonlinear ICA, intrinsic connectivity networks, developmental regression
abstract: This study presents the first comprehensive developmental investigation of whole-brain nonlinear fMRI networks in human infants. A data-driven approach captured brain intrinsic connectivity networks from explicitly nonlinear resting-state fMRI connectivity, profiling their developmental associations in typically developing infants. Neurobiologically structured nonlinear fMRI connectivity patterns were identified during early postnatal life, indicating macroscale brain ensembles systematically participate in nonlinear relationships at birth. Linear and nonlinear network counterparts are linked to partially overlapping but complementary developmental profiles, with the nonlinear approach unveiling insights into sensorimotor, default mode, executive functioning, language production, and stimulus saliency networks.

title: Neonatal sensory networks at birth predict cognitive, language, and motor outcomes at 18 months
authors: Mi Zou, Arun L. W. Bokde
statistical_approach: ROI-degree-guided connectome-based predictive modeling (CPM), Developing Human Connectome Project
abstract: Using data from 402 infants (278 term-born, 124 preterm-born) in the Developing Human Connectome Project, this study introduced an ROI-constrained variant of Connectome-Based Predictive Modeling (CPM) that incorporates ROI-degree-guided feature selection. Models robustly predicted Bayley-III cognitive, language, and motor outcomes at 18 months. Dominant predictive features in term-born infants concentrated in visual-auditory interactions and connections between sensory networks and other cortical regions, identifying early sensory hubs as biomarkers for later neurodevelopmental outcomes.

title: Separable Brain Maturation Patterns Mediate Exposome Influences on Cognitive Development: A Longitudinal Study
authors: Jiadong Yan, Judy Chen, Bin Wan, Paule Joanne Toussaint, Alan C. Evans, Sherif Karama
statistical_approach: Mediation analysis, factor analysis, 4,882 multimodal brain features, 112 exposome variables across six domains
abstract: This longitudinal study examined whether brain maturation mediates the relationship between the exposome and general cognitive ability (g) in a large longitudinal cohort (N=1,112, ages 9-15), using 4,882 multimodal brain features and 112 exposome variables spanning six domains. Factor analysis of domain-level mediation patterns revealed four latent factors, each associated with a largely distinct neural substrate, suggesting that different exposome domains influence cognitive development through partially separable brain mechanisms. The family environment domain showed the strongest correspondence with brain maturation patterns.

title: Instability of Alpha Oscillatory States in Autism and Familial Liability: Evidence from Burst-Resolved High-Density Electroencephalography (EEG)
authors: Theo Vanneau, Chloe Brittenham, Megan Darrell, Michael Quiquempoix, John J. Foxe, Sophie Molholm
institution: Albert Einstein College of Medicine, New York
statistical_approach: Burst-resolved alpha power analysis, time-frequency decomposition, group comparison (non-autistic n=39, autistic n=52, siblings n=26)
abstract: This study investigated the temporal dynamics of alpha oscillations (7-13 Hz) in autistic children (n=52), non-autistic children (n=39), and siblings of autistic children (n=26) aged 8-14 years using burst-resolved high-density EEG. Reduced alpha power in autism was found to reflect reduced temporal stability of alpha bursts rather than reduced burst amplitude. Familial liability was associated with intermediate burst stability between autistic and non-autistic groups, suggesting alpha burst dynamics as a biologically meaningful endophenotype of autism.

title: Cascading periods of language-related brain plasticity across early childhood
authors: Monica E. Ellwood-Lowe, Monami Nishio, Alexander J. Dufford, Michael Arcaro, Theodore D. Satterthwaite, Allyson P. Mackey
statistical_approach: Hurst exponent analysis, Baby Connectome Project (n=222, 458 observations), HCP-Development (n=324), growth curve modeling
abstract: Using two large developmental datasets spanning 10 months to 15 years (Baby Connectome Project and HCP-Development), this study characterized Hurst exponent development in language-related brain regions. Hurst exponent—a measure of neural excitability and plasticity—increases in a graded fashion, with posterior regions developing earlier than anterior regions. Thalamic Hurst plateaus in the first year of life, while cortical Hurst plateaus around age 9. Children with higher language skills showed slower Hurst increases in early childhood, suggesting protracted cortical plasticity supports better language development.

title: Developmental links between play behavior and brain network integration across infancy to middle childhood
authors: Authors from multiple institutions (bioRxiv)
statistical_approach: Resting-state fMRI network analysis, functional connectivity, between-network coupling
abstract: Three studies examined the relationship between play behavior and fMRI-based functional connectivity. Study 1 (N=143, 10 months-3 years): play and imitation behaviors were associated with stronger within-DMN connectivity and DMN-cognitive control network coupling. Study 2 (N=108, 4-11 years): children with more frequent play showed higher DMN-salience and cognitive control-salience connectivity. Study 3 (N=45, 4-12 years): Montessori-educated children showed higher DMN-salience and DMN-cognitive control connectivity vs. traditional schooling. Consistent finding: play is associated with greater default mode, salience, and cognitive control network integration.

title: Functional connectome harmonics capture early brain organization and maturity in neonates
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
statistical_approach: Functional connectome harmonics (FCH), entropy/power/energy metrics, dHCP dataset (714 neonates)
abstract: This study applied functional connectome harmonics (FCH) to resting-state fMRI data from 714 neonates in the Developing Human Connectome Project, mapping early brain organization and maturity. FCH-derived metrics—entropy, power, and energy—were computed from neonatal functional connectivity. Power and energy were higher in term-born compared to preterm neonates; entropy was elevated in preterms. FCH metrics predicted approximately 30% of post-menstrual age variance, suggesting their utility as early markers of brain maturity and prematurity biomarkers.

title: Spectral Geometry of Infant Resting-State fNIRS Connectivity: Bilingual vs Monolingual
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
statistical_approach: Spectral geometry on SPD correlation matrices, JBLD barycentric mean, Grassmann manifold features, leave-one-out validation (n=94)
abstract: This study examined whether bilingual versus monolingual language environments in early infancy are associated with differences in intrinsic functional organization measured from resting-state fNIRS. Using the RS4 infant resting-state fNIRS cohort (N=94), shrinkage-regularized SPD correlation operators and spectral geometry methods were applied. Correlation-based Grassmannian features achieved consistent above-chance separability (ROC-AUC=0.811), with late fusion improving to 0.838 and cross-fusion yielding ROC-AUC of 0.871, suggesting that bilingual exposure induces detectable differences in infant brain network organization.

title: Visual Cortical Response Variability in Infants at High Familial Likelihood for Autism
authors: Abigail Dickinson, Madison Booth, Scott Huberty, Declan Ryan, Alana Campbell, Jessica B. Girault, Neely Miller, Bonnie Lau, John Zempel, Sara Jane Webb, Jed Elison, Adrian K.C. Lee, Annette Estes, Stephen R. Dager, Heather Hazlett, Jason Wolff, Robert Schultz, Natasha Marrus, Alan Evans, Joseph Piven, John R. Pruett Jr., Shafali Jeste
statistical_approach: Visual evoked potential (VEP) P1 latency variability, group comparison, regression on Bayley-III outcomes at 24 months
abstract: Pattern-reversal visual evoked potentials (VEPs) at 6 and 12 months were measured in 177/132 infants with high familial likelihood for autism (IBIS-EP study). P1 latency variability—trial-to-trial consistency—was significantly associated with cognitive and language skills at 24 months. Greater trial-to-trial variability in P1 latency at both timepoints predicted higher cognitive and language scores. Temporal variability in visual cortical response timing emerged as the strongest neural correlate of later developmental outcomes, suggesting adaptive sensory circuit flexibility facilitates experience-dependent tuning.

title: The emergence of the language system in the toddler brain
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
statistical_approach: Univariate fMRI contrasts, left vs. right laterality analysis, Sesame Street audio paradigm (forward vs. reversed)
abstract: Awake fMRI data from 29 toddlers aged 19-36 months during a child-friendly language task (forward vs. backward Sesame Street audio). Left frontal and temporal cortex already responded more to comprehensible language than reversed audio. Although response magnitude was weaker than in adults, brain organization was already adult-like in its left-hemispheric lateralization, challenging claims that frontal language processing does not emerge until later in life. The findings demonstrate that the language system is established in its functional architecture by 19-36 months.

title: From Variability to Synchrony: Non-linear Development of Auditory Neural Responses During the First Year of Life
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
statistical_approach: Inter-trial phase coherence (ITPC), high-density EEG, longitudinal mixed models (2 weeks, 6 months, 12 months, N=69)
abstract: This longitudinal high-density EEG study followed 69 infants at 2 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months using pure tone auditory stimuli. Neural responses became more pronounced with age and showed reduced trial-to-trial variability. Phase synchronization increased from 2 weeks to 6 months in a broad low-frequency range (0-11 Hz). Unexpectedly, synchronization decreased from 6 to 12 months, suggesting a developmental transition toward more differentiated brain activity. These non-linear trajectories indicate dynamic shifts in neural synchronization across the first year of life.

title: Infants have rich visual categories in ventrotemporal cortex at 2 months of age
authors: Cliona O'Doherty, Áine T. Dineen, Anna Truzzi, Graham King, Lorijn Zaadnoordijk, Keelin Harrison, Enna-Louise D'Arcy, Jessica White, Chiara Caldinelli, Tamrin Holloway, Anna Kravchenko, Jörn Diedrichsen, Ailbhe Tarrant, Angela T. Byrne, Adrienne Foran, Eleanor J. Molloy, Rhodri Cusack
statistical_approach: Representational similarity analysis (RSA), deep neural network comparison, multivariate pattern analysis
abstract: This awake fMRI study of over 100 2-month-old infants (with follow-up at 9 months) found that categorical structure is present in high-level visual cortex from 2 months of age. Infants viewed images from 12 familiar visual categories while brain activity was recorded. Category representations in ventrotemporal cortex were similar to adult representations, including animate/inanimate distinctions. A deep neural network model aligned with infants' representational geometry, indicating early category templates can be learned from statistics of visual input. Categorical structure precedes its emergence in lateral visual cortex, suggesting non-hierarchical development.

title: Explainable AI uncovers novel EEG microstate candidate neurophysiological markers for autism spectrum disorder
authors: Delna Kuriyakose, Gowsalya M.
statistical_approach: EEG microstates, XGBoost classifier (80.87% accuracy), SHAP explainability, multi-domain feature extraction (temporal, spectral, complexity, graph-theoretic)
abstract: This study proposes a novel multi-domain analytical pipeline extracting and integrating EEG microstate features from four domains: temporal dynamics (microstate duration, transition entropy), spectral power within microstate-defined segments, complexity metrics (permutation entropy, fractal dimension), and graph-theoretical measures from microstate transition matrices. XGBoost achieved 80.87% classification accuracy for ASD vs. neurotypical. SHAP-based interpretability identified fractional occupancy derivative for microstate 3, delta-band power, and mean inter-transition interval as top discriminative features, providing interpretable neurophysiological biomarkers for ASD.

title: Functional connectivity in infants' visual cortex and its links to motion processing and autism
authors: Irzam Hardiansyah, Giorgia Bussu, Sven Bölte, Emily J. H. Jones, Terje Falck-Ytter
statistical_approach: EEG functional connectivity (theta, alpha, gamma), social scene viewing paradigm, EASE cohort (N=59, 39 elevated familial likelihood for autism)
abstract: This study assessed 5-month-old infants (n=59; 39 with elevated familial likelihood of autism) with EEG while they watched videos showing social and non-social scenes from the Early Autism in Sweden (EASE) cohort. Gamma connectivity between midline and far-lateral visual cortex during social scene viewing was linked to both later autism symptoms and global motion visual cortical laterality. The results suggest that altered early visual cortical functional connectivity may represent an EEG biomarker linking visual motion processing atypicalities to later autism symptomatology.

title: The Fronto-Temporal Cortex Has Increased Subcortical Connectivity In Utero and Plasticity in Adulthood
authors: Gabriela Epihova, Dimitar Z. Epihov, Danyal Akarca, Duncan E. Astle
institution: MRC Cognition and Brain Sciences Unit, University of Cambridge
statistical_approach: Fetal MRI connectivity analysis, gene expression annotation, perineuronal net markers, myelination quantification
abstract: At 24 postgestational weeks, fronto-temporal regions are disproportionately connected to subcortical regions, highlighting their role in early integrative cortical-subcortical communication. In adulthood, fronto-temporal cortex shows lower myelin content and higher expression of undifferentiated progenitor cell markers, as well as lower expression of perineuronal net marker genes, suggesting a heightened state of plasticity. This finding suggests fronto-temporal regions evolved extended developmental timelines to maximize flexible neural responses during postnatal experience.

title: Deciphering multiway multiscale brain network connectivity from birth to 6 months
authors: Qiang Li, Zening Fu, Hasse Walum, Masoud Seraji, Prerana Bajracharya, Vince D. Calhoun, Sarah Shultz, Armin Iraji
institution: Georgia State University / Emory University / TReNDS Center
statistical_approach: High-order triadic interactions, pairwise vs. beyond-pairwise connectivity, 71 infants (126 scans), functional network connectivity
abstract: This study characterized multiway multiscale brain network interactions in 71 typically developing infants during their first six months of life. High-order triadic interactions revealed significant hierarchical, multiway, multiscale brain functional network interactions beyond pairwise connectivity. These triadic interactions predominantly involve the default mode, sensorimotor, visual, limbic, language, salience, and central executive domains—aligning with the classical triple network model—suggesting the core brain network architecture is initially established during the first six months of infancy. High-order interactions provided more reliable biomarkers of developmental trajectories than pairwise measures.

title: Hierarchical maturation of structural brain connectomes from birth to childhood
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
statistical_approach: Connectome mapping, machine learning, neurobiological annotation, network efficiency metrics
abstract: This study reveals hierarchical development of the brain's structural connectome from infancy to childhood using connectome mapping, machine learning, and neurobiological annotation. Two cardinal topological transformations occur: (1) nonlinear global increases in network efficiency and robustness; (2) regional reorganization with accelerated hub consolidation and prolonged modular reconfiguration in prefrontal and insular cortices. Early developmental trajectories of association cortices predict late childhood network architecture through local microstructural maturation of connected white matter tracts, characterized by distinct sensorimotor-association developmental trajectories.

title: Resting state aperiodic and periodic EEG activity in preschool-aged autistic children: differences from neurotypical peers and links to language skills
authors: Yanru Chen, Meagan Tsou, Charles A. Nelson, Helen Tager-Flusberg, Carol L. Wilkinson
institution: Boston Children's Hospital, Harvard Medical School
statistical_approach: Spectral parameterization (specparam/FOOOF), aperiodic exponent/offset, periodic peaks, group comparison (64 ASD, 64 neurotypical, ages 2-6), regression on language skills
abstract: This study examined resting-state aperiodic and periodic EEG features in 64 neurotypical and 64 autistic children aged 2-6 years. Aperiodic and periodic features from absolute power spectra were compared between groups and linked to concurrent language skills, with a subgroup analysis of children with language impairment. Both aperiodic and periodic EEG features showed group differences between autistic and neurotypical preschoolers, and were significantly associated with concurrent language ability. The findings suggest spectral EEG components may reflect neural mechanisms underlying language development in autism.

title: Simultaneous EEG-fMRI investigation of sound sequence processing in human neonates
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
statistical_approach: EEG mismatch response analysis, fMRI BOLD contrasts, auditory oddball paradigm (n=25 full-term neonates, 22 analyzable)
abstract: This study acquired simultaneous EEG and fMRI data in 25 full-term sleeping neonates during an auditory oddball paradigm investigating sound sequence processing. EEG mismatch response was identified for deviant sounds but not omissions, suggesting neonates encode local but not global sequence regularity. fMRI revealed distributed cortical activity including superior and ventromedial temporal lobe (hippocampus, parahippocampus), precentral gyrus, thalamus, and parahippocampal cortex. This simultaneous multimodal approach provides comprehensive spatial and temporal insight into sensory deviance detection in the neonatal brain.

title: Aperiodic parameters of the fMRI power spectrum associate with preterm birth and neonatal age
authors: [UNVERIFIED — authors not resolved from search snippets]
statistical_approach: Aperiodic BOLD signal parameterization (0.01-0.15 Hz), 90 cortical/subcortical ROIs, machine learning regression, dHCP dataset
abstract: This study parameterized aperiodic components of resting-state fMRI BOLD signal power spectra from 90 cortical and subcortical regions of interest in neonates from the Developing Human Connectome Project. Pre- and postcentral gyri BOLD aperiodic exponent and offset were significantly associated with preterm birth and post-menstrual age. Machine learning analyses showed R² of 0.20-0.41 between aperiodic fMRI parameters and neonatal age, demonstrating feasibility for studying functional brain maturation via fMRI-based aperiodic activity.

title: Singing to the newborn brain uncovers early traces of specialized neural networks
authors: Marino, C., Gemignani, J., Varnet, L., and co-authors
statistical_approach: fNIRS hemodynamic response analysis, within-subject contrasts (speech vs. song vs. humming), N=40 newborns
abstract: This fNIRS study with 40 newborns examined neural responses to speech, song, and humming. Stronger responses for both speech and song (vs. humming) were found in right temporoparietal regions, demonstrating early traces of specialized neural networks for communication. No significant neural differences were found between speech and song despite clear acoustic distinctions, consistent with the hypothesis that song, by regularizing speech rhythm, may support language acquisition in early development.

title: Regional BOLD variability reflects microstructural maturation and neuronal ensheathment in the preterm infant cortex
authors: Sa de Almeida, J., Boehringer, A., Loukas, S., and co-authors
statistical_approach: Longitudinal rs-fMRI BOLD variability analysis, multi-shell diffusion MRI, gene expression analysis, N=54 preterm infants + 24 full-term
abstract: This longitudinal study combined rs-fMRI and multi-shell diffusion imaging in 54 very preterm infants (at 33 weeks GA and term-equivalent age) and 24 full-term newborns. During preterm development, BOLD variability increased in primary sensory-sensorimotor and proto-DMN regions, accompanied by decreased cortical diffusivity. Gene expression analysis revealed concurrent upregulation of gliogenesis and neuronal ensheathment genes. By term age, preterm infants showed immature cortical maturation patterns compared with full-term newborns, linking BOLD variability to ongoing microstructural maturation.

title: Changes in Early Aperiodic EEG Activity Are Linked to Autism Diagnosis and Language Development in Infants With Family History of Autism
authors: Carol L. Wilkinson, Haerin Chung, Amy Dave, Helen Tager-Flusberg, Charles A. Nelson
institution: Boston Children's Hospital, Harvard Medical School
statistical_approach: Longitudinal EEG aperiodic change (3-12 months), regression on ASD diagnosis and 18-month language outcomes
abstract: This study examined longitudinal changes in aperiodic EEG activity from 3 to 12 months in infants with elevated familial likelihood for autism. Infants with a later autism diagnosis showed significantly increased aperiodic activity change compared to elevated-familial-likelihood infants without diagnosis. Greater increases in aperiodic offset and slope from 3-12 months were associated with worse 18-month language development, even after controlling for autism diagnosis. These results suggest dynamic changes in aperiodic EEG serve as early indicators of autism and language outcomes in high-risk infants.

title: Resting-state periodic and aperiodic brain oscillations from birth to preschool years: Aperiodic maturity predicts developmental course
authors: Heather L Green, James Christopher Edgar, Kylie Mol, Marybeth McNamee, Laura Prosser, Mina Kim, Emily S Kuschner, Gregory A Miller, Yuhan Chen
institution: Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, Department of Radiology
statistical_approach: Specparam (FOOOF), power spectrum analysis, nonlinear age modeling, mixed-effects models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study characterizes the developmental trajectory of resting-state periodic and aperiodic brain oscillations from birth through the preschool years using MEG. Applying the Specparam (FOOOF) algorithm, the authors separately model aperiodic (1/f) and periodic (oscillatory) components of the power spectrum across a broad age range (2–68 months). Findings show that aperiodic maturity — the degree to which the aperiodic exponent approaches adult-like values — predicts subsequent developmental course, suggesting it as a biomarker of neural maturation.
key_figure: Aperiodic exponent developmental trajectories from birth to preschool with age-nonlinear curve fits

title: Dynamics of the attentional blink in preverbal infants
authors: F. Leroy, S. Naik, R. Gulbinaite, M. Palu, D. Battaglia, G. Dehaene-Lambertz
institution: CEA (Commissariat à l'énergie atomique et aux énergies alternatives), France
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
statistical_approach: Saccade analysis, pupillometry, steady-state EEG frequency-tagging, temporal dynamics modeling
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study examines whether preverbal 4-month-old infants exhibit the attentional blink — a temporal limitation in visual attention where a second target presented shortly after a first target is often missed. Using combined EEG and eye-tracking methods with steady-state visual evoked potential paradigms, the authors demonstrate that infants show attention dynamics analogous to the attentional blink seen in adults, revealing early-emerging constraints on temporal attention in the developing brain.
key_figure: Attentional blink curves (detection accuracy vs. inter-target lag) in 4-month-old infants compared to adult benchmarks

title: Tracing infant sleep neurophysiology longitudinally from 3 to 6 months: EEG insights into brain development
authors: Matthieu Beaugrand, Valeria Jaramillo, Christophe Mühlematter, Sarah F. Schoch, Vivien Reicher, Andjela Markovic, Salome Kurth
institution: University of Surrey, Surrey Sleep Research Centre
statistical_approach: EEG power spectral analysis, topographic mapping, longitudinal mixed-effects models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This longitudinal study traces changes in infant sleep neurophysiology using EEG from 3 to 6 months of age. By analyzing EEG power across frequency bands during sleep, the authors characterize developmental changes in sleep architecture and brain oscillatory activity across this key window of early brain development, providing normative benchmarks for sleep EEG maturation.
key_figure: Topographic maps of EEG power across sleep stages at 3 and 6 months

title: Unpredictable caregiving is associated with disrupted neurophysiological measures of attention and autonomic function in three-month-old infants
authors: Annie Brandes-Aitken, Maya Metser, Stephen H. Braren, Sarah C. Vogel, Natalie H. Brito
institution: New York University, Department of Applied Psychology
statistical_approach: Frontal theta power analysis, heart rate variability (HRV) analysis, regression models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study examines how unpredictable caregiving environments are associated with neurophysiological markers of attention and autonomic regulation in 3-month-old infants. EEG measures of frontal theta power and heart rate variability (HRV) are used to index attentional and autonomic function. Results indicate that infants exposed to more unpredictable caregiving show disrupted patterns of neural attention and autonomic regulation, highlighting early neural consequences of caregiving adversity.
key_figure: Correlation between caregiving unpredictability scores and infant frontal theta power/HRV metrics

title: Effects of Early Infant Nutrition on Aperiodic Exponent From 2 to 12 Months Using EEG Analysis
authors: Dylan Gilbreath, Adam Andrews, Darcy Hagood, Aline Andres, Linda J Larson-Prior
institution: University of Arkansas for Medical Sciences
statistical_approach: Power spectral density (Specparam/FOOOF), mixed-effects models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study examines how early infant nutrition (breastfeeding vs. formula) influences the EEG aperiodic exponent (1/f slope) from 2 to 12 months of age. Using power spectral density analysis with the Specparam toolbox, the authors track longitudinal changes in aperiodic EEG activity, a marker of excitation/inhibition balance. Results indicate nutrition-related differences in the developmental trajectory of the aperiodic exponent across early infancy.
key_figure: Longitudinal aperiodic exponent trajectories by feeding group across 2, 6, and 12-month timepoints

title: Cross-paradigm fNIRS brain activity in 1-month-old infants across The Gambia and the United Kingdom
authors: Isobel Greenhalgh, Borja Blanco, Chiara Bulgarelli, Ebrima Mbye, Ebou Touray, Maria Rozhko, Laura Katus, Nathan Hayes, Samantha McCann, Sophie E. Moore, Clare E. Elwell, Anna Blasi, Sarah Lloyd-Fox
statistical_approach: fNIRS channel-level analysis, cross-cultural comparison, linear mixed models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This cross-cultural study examines fNIRS-measured brain activity in 1-month-old infants across two markedly different environments — The Gambia and the United Kingdom. Using multiple paradigms (social selectivity, habituation/novelty, and functional connectivity), the authors characterize neural responses to social and non-social stimuli across these culturally and environmentally distinct populations. Findings reveal both universal patterns and cross-cultural differences in early brain function, contributing to understanding of how environment shapes early neural development.
key_figure: fNIRS activation maps across paradigms for Gambian vs. UK infant cohorts

title: Income insufficiency impacts early brain development in infants facing increased psychosocial adversity: A network-based approach
authors: Haerin Chung, Carol L. Wilkinson, A. Liu, A. Job Said, B. Francis, G. Cañaveral, K. Conroy, Helen Tager-Flusberg, Charles A. Nelson
institution: Boston Children's Hospital, Harvard Medical School
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
statistical_approach: Partial correlation network analysis, longitudinal EEG power analysis, graph-theoretic network metrics
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study uses a network-based approach to examine how income insufficiency and psychosocial adversity affect early brain development in infants. Longitudinal EEG data from 4 to 12 months reveals that income-related adversity is associated with altered resting-state oscillatory network topology, with implications for understanding the neural mechanisms by which socioeconomic factors shape infant brain development.
key_figure: Network connectivity matrices comparing income-sufficient versus income-insufficient groups across development

title: Language exposure predicts infants' neural processing of others' actions based on language group
authors: Yiyi Wang, Marc Colomer, Hyesung Grace Hwang, Enda Tan, Nathan A Fox
statistical_approach: Mu desynchronization analysis, frontal theta power, frontal alpha asymmetry, mixed-effects models
abstract: [UNVERIFIED — from search snippet] This study investigates how early language exposure shapes infants' neural responses to others' goal-directed actions. Using EEG with 8- to 12-month-old infants from different language groups, the authors measure mu desynchronization (motor resonance), frontal theta, and frontal alpha asymmetry during action observation. Results indicate that language group moderates neural processing of actions, suggesting that early linguistic experience influences the neural systems underlying social cognition and action understanding.
key_figure: EEG mu desynchronization topographic maps by language group during action observation
